title: Why do we keep building monuments to people who didn't ask for them?
summary: Explore the unease of statues, plaques, and memorials raised to figures who never consented.
topic: Why we keep building monuments to people who didn't ask for them
prompt: Write an essay (350–500 words) exploring why societies keep raising statues and monuments to figures who never asked to be commemorated. Take a clear position. Use at least one cleft sentence, one concession, and one international example (Bristol's Colston, Budapest's Memento Park, Cape Town's Rhodes).
hintStarters: When the statue of Edward Colston was toppled in Bristol in 2020…
What a monument really claims is…
Granted, every generation has the right to honour its dead.
Perhaps the deeper unease is that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: tượng đài / công trình tưởng niệm
làm phẳng / đơn giản hóa một cuộc đời
sự lưỡng lự / mâu thuẫn nội tâm

sampleText:
When the statue of Edward Colston was toppled in Bristol in 2020 and rolled into the harbour, the most interesting argument was not whether he deserved to fall, but whether he had ever asked to stand. Colston, a seventeenth-century slave trader, died in 1721. The bronze figure was raised in 1895 by people who needed a local hero more than they needed an honest one. The man on the pedestal was a posthumous invention, and the city had spent a century calling it him.

What a monument really claims is not that someone was great, but that the present generation has the right to decide who looms over the next. That is a strange power. The dead cannot edit their image, refuse a plaque, or step down from a pedestal. A statue tends to flatten a life into a single attitude — usually a forward gaze, a raised hand, a confident jaw — and the longer the bronze stands, the more it convinces the city that the life behind it was simple. It rarely was.

Granted, every generation has the right to honour its dead, and to refuse to do so would be a different kind of forgetting. Budapest's Memento Park, where Soviet-era statues were gathered after 1989 rather than melted down, suggests a useful middle path: the past is not erased, but it is no longer enshrined. The visitor sees a Lenin, but on flat ground, surrounded by other Lenins, drained of authority. The lesson is not that the figures never existed, but that no one has to keep looking up at them.

What's more, monuments increasingly carry an asymmetry the original sculptors never imagined: the subject's life is finished, but our reading of it is not. Cecil Rhodes, whose statue at the University of Cape Town was removed in 2015, may well have considered himself a civilising figure. The students of 2015 did not, and they did not have to. The monument outlived the consensus it once expressed, and the city was left holding a bronze argument the subject could no longer defend.

Perhaps the deeper unease is this: a permanent statue assumes a permanent verdict, and we no longer believe in permanent verdicts. It seems to me that the honest response is not to stop commemorating, but to commemorate with humility — smaller plaques, removable inscriptions, public histories that can be edited as we learn more. A city does not owe its founders a forever pedestal. It owes its inhabitants the freedom to disagree about them.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: một sự sáng tạo sau khi đã qua đời
điều mà một tượng đài thật sự khẳng định
chỉnh sửa hình ảnh của mình
đơn giản hóa một cuộc đời

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Granted, every generation has the right
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: công nhận, mỗi thế hệ đều có quyền
tồn tại lâu hơn sự đồng thuận
rất có thể đã tự coi mình

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the subject could no longer defend
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: đối tượng không còn có thể bảo vệ được nữa
tưởng niệm một cách khiêm tốn
khắc chữ có thể tháo dỡ

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft for defining a concept
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a monument really claims is not that someone was great, but that the present generation has the right…' dùng để định nghĩa lại một khái niệm và đẩy thông tin chính ra cuối câu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a monument really claims is not that … but that …' uses a what-cleft to redefine a concept, contrasting two ideas at the end of the sentence.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal of probability for hedged historical claims
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Cecil Rhodes may well have considered himself a civilising figure' dùng 'may well + have + V-PP' để đoán định một điều có thể đã đúng trong quá khứ — một cách tránh nói tuyệt đối.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'May well have + past participle' is a hedged modal for past probability — useful when you're guessing about historical figures' views without claiming certainty.

sampleTranslationVi:
Khi tượng Edward Colston bị lật đổ ở Bristol năm 2020 và bị lăn xuống cảng, lập luận thú vị nhất không phải là liệu ông có đáng bị đổ hay không, mà là ông có bao giờ xin được đứng đó không. Colston, một thương nhân buôn nô lệ thế kỷ mười bảy, qua đời năm 1721. Tượng đồng được dựng năm 1895 bởi những người cần một anh hùng địa phương nhiều hơn là cần một anh hùng thành thật. Người đàn ông trên bệ tượng là một sáng tạo sau khi đã qua đời, và thành phố đã dành một thế kỷ để gọi đó là ông ta.

Điều mà một tượng đài thật sự khẳng định không phải là ai đó vĩ đại, mà là thế hệ hiện tại có quyền quyết định ai sẽ phủ bóng lên thế hệ kế tiếp. Đó là một quyền lực kỳ lạ. Người chết không thể chỉnh sửa hình ảnh của mình, không thể từ chối một tấm biển, không thể bước xuống khỏi một bệ tượng. Một bức tượng có xu hướng làm phẳng một cuộc đời thành một thái độ duy nhất — thường là ánh nhìn về phía trước, cánh tay giơ lên, cằm tự tin — và càng đứng lâu, tượng đồng càng thuyết phục thành phố rằng cuộc đời phía sau nó là đơn giản. Hiếm khi nó đơn giản.

Công nhận, mỗi thế hệ đều có quyền tôn vinh người đã khuất, và từ chối làm vậy sẽ là một kiểu lãng quên khác. Memento Park ở Budapest, nơi các tượng thời Liên Xô được tập hợp lại sau năm 1989 thay vì bị nấu chảy, gợi ý một con đường trung gian hữu ích: quá khứ không bị xóa, nhưng cũng không còn được thiêng liêng hóa. Người tham quan thấy một Lenin, nhưng trên mặt đất phẳng, vây quanh bởi những Lenin khác, bị tước đi uy quyền. Bài học không phải là các nhân vật đó chưa từng tồn tại, mà là không ai phải tiếp tục ngước nhìn lên họ.

Hơn nữa, các tượng đài ngày càng mang một sự bất đối xứng mà các nhà điêu khắc ban đầu không hề tưởng tượng: cuộc đời của đối tượng đã kết thúc, nhưng cách chúng ta đọc nó thì chưa. Cecil Rhodes, người mà tượng đặt tại Đại học Cape Town đã bị dỡ năm 2015, rất có thể đã tự coi mình là một nhân vật khai hóa. Sinh viên năm 2015 thì không, và họ không phải nghĩ vậy. Tượng đài đã sống lâu hơn sự đồng thuận từng tạo ra nó, và thành phố bị bỏ lại với một lập luận bằng đồng mà đối tượng không còn có thể bảo vệ được nữa.

Có lẽ sự bất an sâu hơn là thế này: một bức tượng vĩnh viễn giả định một phán quyết vĩnh viễn, và chúng ta không còn tin vào những phán quyết vĩnh viễn. Tôi thấy rằng phản ứng trung thực không phải là ngừng tưởng niệm, mà là tưởng niệm một cách khiêm tốn — các tấm biển nhỏ hơn, khắc chữ có thể tháo dỡ, lịch sử công cộng có thể được chỉnh sửa khi chúng ta hiểu thêm. Một thành phố không nợ những người sáng lập một bệ tượng mãi mãi. Nó nợ cư dân của mình tự do không đồng tình về họ.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option opens an essay with a concrete event rather than a cliché?
options: In today's modern society, statues are a hot topic.
Throughout history, monuments have always been controversial.

When the statue of Edward Colston was toppled in Bristol in 2020 and rolled into the harbour, the most interesting argument was not whether he deserved to fall, but whether he had ever asked to stand.
It is important to note that statues exist in many cities.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C grounds the essay in a specific year, city, and image — the others are AI-style generalities.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the option with a date, place, and image.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which what-cleft puts the central claim at the end of the sentence?
options: What a monument really claims is not that someone was great, but that the present generation has the right to decide who looms over the next.
A monument what claims really is great someone not the present generation.
It is what a monument that really claims great someone present generation.
Really a monument claims what is the right great someone.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X claims is not Y but Z' is a clean what-cleft contrasting two ideas at the end.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'tends to' as a hedge most naturally?
options: A statue tends to flatten a life into a single attitude.
Tends to a statue flatten a life single attitude.
A statue is tend flatten a life into single attitude.
Statues tend flattening a life into one attitude single.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tends to + base verb' hedges the generalisation appropriately.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which concession most naturally precedes a counter-move?
options: Granted, every generation has the right to honour its dead, and to refuse to do so would be a different kind of forgetting.
Of course granted every generation has the right dead honour their which.
Conceding which to honour the dead generation every has right to forgetting.
Although granted every generation dead honour the right has it of forgetting.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, X. + counter-clause' is a clean B2 concession.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'Granted,' followed by a fluent clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'may well have' (past probability) correctly?
options: Cecil Rhodes may well have considered himself a civilising figure.
Cecil Rhodes may have well considering himself civilising.
Rhodes well may consider himself civilising have been.
May Rhodes well be considered civilising himself have.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'May well have + past participle' is the correct hedged past-probability structure.
mcQuestions.hint: Subject + may well have + past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best continues the previous sentence ending '…drained of authority'?
options: Throughout history, authority drains from many things.
The lesson is not that the figures never existed, but that no one has to keep looking up at them.
It is important to note that authority can be drained.
In conclusion, draining authority is generally good.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B continues the thread by stating the lesson the previous sentence implied.
mcQuestions.hint: Which one continues the actual argument with concrete content?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'outlived' in the correct collocation?
options: The monument outlived the consensus it once expressed.
The monument out-live a consensus once expressing it.
Outlived the monument that consensus expressing once.
A monument has outlive the consensus express it once.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'X outlived Y' is the natural past-tense collocation meaning X persisted longer than Y.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which cohesive marker most naturally introduces an additional point about asymmetry?
options: Whatever anyway,
What's more, monuments increasingly carry an asymmetry the original sculptors never imagined.
On the contrary which moreover,
Furthermore in addition besides moreover,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What's more' is a clean cohesive marker that adds a related point.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the cleanest, most natural connector.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a relative clause correctly?
options: Cecil Rhodes, whose statue at the University of Cape Town was removed in 2015, may well have considered himself a civilising figure.
Cecil Rhodes which statue removed Cape Town 2015 may consider himself.
Cecil Rhodes that statue was removed in 2015 University of Cape Town may.
Cecil Rhodes the statue Cape Town removed 2015 may which civilising.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whose statue …' attaches a possessive relative clause correctly to the subject.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the natural possessive relative form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence avoids moralising in its ending?
options: We must all learn to respect history and our great founders.
Throughout history, humans have always built monuments.
A city does not owe its founders a forever pedestal. It owes its inhabitants the freedom to disagree about them.
In conclusion, monuments are a difficult problem and we should think about them.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C ends with a precise contrast inside the argument, avoiding both sermons and platitudes.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the ending that finishes the argument, not preaches it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which subtle register choice is best for an essay arguing about commemoration?
options: Statues r kinda creepy ngl.
Statues are sometimes super weird, you know?
The man on the pedestal was a posthumous invention.
Lol that statue got rekt in the harbour.
mcQuestions.explanation: Only C keeps the formal-but-concrete register of a B2 essay.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'rarely' with inversion correctly?
options: Rarely was the life behind the bronze actually simple.
Rarely it was the life simple behind the bronze.
Rarely the life was simple bronze behind.
It rarely the bronze life simple behind was.
mcQuestions.explanation: Inversion after 'rarely' requires 'rarely + auxiliary + subject + main verb'.

options: to enshrine the past
to enshrine a sandwich
to enshrine a delivery
to enshrine a kerb
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Enshrine' collocates with abstract things like 'rights', 'memory', 'the past' — not concrete everyday objects.
mcQuestions.hint: Which one matches 'enshrine' with an abstract noun?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'no longer' correctly to mark a change?
options: The past is not erased, but it is no longer enshrined.
The past no longer it erased is, but enshrined still.
Erased no longer the past, but enshrined yet is.
Past it is no longer erased enshrined but still.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is no longer + past participle' is the natural way to express a discontinued state.
mcQuestions.hint: Subject + is + no longer + adjective/past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'seems to me' as a hedge correctly?
options: Seems to me the honest response is commemorating with humility.
It seems to me that the honest response is not to stop commemorating, but to commemorate with humility.
It seem to me the honest response stops commemorating not but with humility.
Seem me to it the response honest commemorating humility.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It seems to me that + clause' is the standard hedged-opinion structure.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the cleanest 'It seems to me that …'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Choose one statue you've walked past. If you had to write a one-paragraph plaque the subject would object to but the city deserves, what would it say?
